questions.subject: Physics
questions.examName: Most Important Selected Qs for JEE Advanced
questions.q: Paragraph:If the velocity of a charged particle moving through a uniform magnetic field has a component parallel to that field the particle will move in a helical path as shown in figure 1. The net force F on the particle can be described by the equation. $\mathrm{F}=\mathrm{qvB} \sin \theta$ where q is the charge on the particle, v is the velocity of the charge, $B$ is the magnetic field strength, and $\theta$ is the angle between the velocity and the magnetic field. The pitch $p$ of the helix is the distance between adjacent turns. The radius $r$ of the helix is determined by the component of velocity of the charge perpendicular to the magnetic field and is independent of the component of the velocity parallel to the magnetic field. A nonuniform magnetic field that is stronger at its ends than at its middle can trap a charged particle by reflecting it back and forth between ends. Such a situation is shown in figure 2 . In a similar phenomenon electrons and protons above the atmosphere are reflected back and forth between the north and south poles of the earth's magnetic field forming the two Van Allen belts high above the atmosphere. Occasionally a solar flare shoots additional electrons and protons into the Van Allen belts. This pushes the electrons to a lesser height. The repulsion due to additional electrons drives the charged particles along the earth's magnetic field lines into the atmosphere where they collide with the air molecules forcing them to a higher energy level emitting photons. Oxygen atoms emit green light and nitrogen atoms emit pink. This light forms the curtain of lights in the sky known in the northern hemisphere as the aurora borealisQuestion:If the magnetic field in figure-2 represents a portion of the Earth's magnetic field, at which labelled point would you except to find the aurora borealis?
questions.solution: Geographical north of earth is south pole of the earth's magnetism
questions.source: Most Important Selected Qs for JEE Advanced

questions.subject: Physics
questions.examName: Most Important Selected Qs for JEE Advanced
questions.q: Paragraph:Analog voltmeters and ammeters work by measuring the torque exerted by a magnetic field on a current carrying coil. The reading is displayed by means of the deflection of a pointer over a scale. The adjacent figure shows the essentials of a galvanometer, on which both analog ammeters and analog voltmeters are based. Assume that the coil is 2.1 cm high, 1.2 cm wide, has 250 turns and is mounted so that it can rotate about an axis (into the page) in a uniform radial magnetic field with $\mathrm{B}=0.23 \mathrm{~T}$. For any orientation of the coil, the net magnetic field through the coil is perpendicular to the normal vector of the coil (and thus parallel to the plane of coil). A spring $\mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{P}}$ provides a counter torque that balances the magnetic torque, so that a given steady current I in the coil results in a steady angular deflection $\phi$. The greater the current is, greater the deflection is, and thus greater the torque required of the spring is. A current of $100 \mu \mathrm{~A}$ produces an angular deflection of $28^{\circ}$.Question:What must be the torsional constant K of the spring?
questions.source: Most Important Selected Qs for JEE Advanced

questions.q: Paragraph:If the velocity of a charged particle moving through a uniform magnetic field has a component parallel to that field the particle will move in a helical path as shown in figure 1. The net force F on the particle can be described by the equation. $\mathrm{F}=\mathrm{qvB} \sin \theta$ where q is the charge on the particle, v is the velocity of the charge, $B$ is the magnetic field strength, and $\theta$ is the angle between the velocity and the magnetic field. The radius $r$ of the helix is determined by the component of velocity of the charge perpendicular to the magnetic field and is independent of the component of the velocity parallel to the magnetic field. A nonuniform magnetic field that is stronger at its ends than at its middle can trap a charged particle by reflecting it back and forth between ends. Such a situation is shown in figure 2 . In a similar phenomenon electrons and protons above the atmosphere are reflected back and forth between the north and south poles of the earth's magnetic field forming the two Van Allen belts high above the atmosphere. Occasionally a solar flare shoots additional electrons and protons into the Van Allen belts. This pushes the electrons to a lesser height. The repulsion due to additional electrons drives the charged particles along the earth's magnetic field lines into the atmosphere where they collide with the air molecules forcing them to a higher energy level emitting photons. Oxygen atoms emit green light and nitrogen atoms emit pink. This light forms the curtain of lights in the sky known in the northern hemisphere as the aurora borealisQuestion:The electrons and protons caught in the Van Allen belt reflect from one pole to the other. Looking up from the North pole, if electrons rotate counterclockwise then protons rotate
options: counterclockwise both when looking up from the north pole or when looking up from the south pole.
clockwise both when looking up from the north pole of when looking up from the south pole.
clockwise when looking up from the north pole but counterclockwise when looking up from the south pole.
counterclockwise when looking up from the north pole but clockwise when looking up from the south pole.
questions.solution: If electron rotates counterclockwise, proton will rotate clockwise if seen from noth pole.
questions.source: Most Important Selected Qs for JEE Advanced

questions.subject: Physics
questions.examName: Most Important Selected Qs for JEE Advanced
questions.q: Paragraph:If the velocity of a charged particle moving through a uniform magnetic field has a component parallel to that field the particle will move in a helical path as shown in figure 1. The net force F on the particle can be described by the equation. $\mathrm{F}=\mathrm{qvB} \sin \theta$ where q is the charge on the particle, v is the velocity of the charge, $B$ is the magnetic field strength, and $\theta$ is the angle between the velocity and the magnetic field. The pitch $p$ of the helix is the distance between adjacent turns. The radius $r$ of the helix is determined by the component of velocity of the charge perpendicular to the magnetic field and is independent of the component of the velocity parallel to the magnetic field. A nonuniform magnetic field that is stronger at its ends than at its middle can trap a charged particle by reflecting it back and forth between ends. Such a situation is shown in figure 2 . In a similar phenomenon electrons and protons above the atmosphere are reflected back and forth between the north and south poles of the earth's magnetic field forming the two Van Allen belts high above the atmosphere. Occasionally a solar flare shoots additional electrons and protons into the Van Allen belts. This pushes the electrons to a lesser height. The repulsion due to additional electrons drives the charged particles along the earth's magnetic field lines into the atmosphere where they collide with the air molecules forcing them to a higher energy level emitting photons. Oxygen atoms emit green light and nitrogen atoms emit pink. This light forms the curtain of lights in the sky known in the northern hemisphere as the aurora borealisQuestion:Which of the following most accurately describes the change in the pitch and period of the helical path traveled by a negatively charged particle moving through a magnetic field that is gradually increasing?
options: The pitch increases and the period decreases
The pitch increases and the period increases
The pitch decreases and the period remains the same
Both the pitch and the period decrease.
questions.solution: $\begin{aligned} & \text { Pitch }=\mathrm{V}_{\|} \times \text {time to complete one rotation }=\mathrm{V}_{\|} \times \frac{2 \pi m}{q B} \\ & \text { Period of rotation }=\frac{2 \pi m}{q B}\end{aligned}$
questions.source: Most Important Selected Qs for JEE Advanced

questions.subject: Physics
questions.examName: Most Important Selected Qs for JEE Advanced
questions.q: Paragraph:As a charged particle q moving with a velocity $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{v}}$ enters a uniform magnetic field $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{B}}$, it experiences a force $\vec{f}=\mathrm{q}(\overrightarrow{\mathrm{v}} \times \overrightarrow{\mathrm{B}})$. For $\theta=0^{\circ}$ or $180^{\circ}, \theta$ being the angle between $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{v}}$ and $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{B}}$, force experienced is zero and the particle passes undeflected. For $\theta=90^{\circ}$, the particle moves along a circular arc and the magnetic force (qvB) provides the necessary centripetal force $\left(\frac{\mathrm{mv}^2}{r}\right)$. For other values of $\theta\left(\theta \neq 0^{\circ}\right.$, $180^{\circ}, 90^{\circ}$ ), the charged particle moves along a helical path which is the resultant motion of simultaneous circular and translational motions. Suppose a particle, that carries a charge of magnitude q and has a mass $4 \times 10^{-15} \mathrm{~kg}$, is moving in a region containing a uniform magnetic field $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{B}}=-0.4 \hat{\mathrm{k}} \mathrm{T}$. At a certain instant, velocity of the particle is $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{v}}=(8 \hat{\mathrm{i}}-6 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+4 \hat{\mathrm{k}}) \times 10^6 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$ and force acting on it has a magnitude 1.6 N . Answer the following :Question:Which of the three components of acceleration have non-zero values?
questions.source: Most Important Selected Qs for JEE Advanced

questions.subject: Physics
questions.examName: Most Important Selected Qs for JEE Advanced
questions.q: Paragraph:As a charged particle q moving with a velocity $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{v}}$ enters a uniform magnetic field $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{B}}$, it experiences a force $\vec{f}=\mathrm{q}(\overrightarrow{\mathrm{v}} \times \overrightarrow{\mathrm{B}})$. For $\theta=0^{\circ}$ or $180^{\circ}, \theta$ being the angle between $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{v}}$ and $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{B}}$, force experienced is zero and the particle passes undeflected. For $\theta=90^{\circ}$, the particle moves along a circular arc and the magnetic force (qvB) provides the necessary centripetal force $\left(\frac{\mathrm{mv}^2}{r}\right)$. For other values of $\theta\left(\theta \neq 0^{\circ}\right.$, $180^{\circ}, 90^{\circ}$ ), the charged particle moves along a helical path which is the resultant motion of simultaneous circular and translational motions. Suppose a particle, that carries a charge of magnitude q and has a mass $4 \times 10^{-15} \mathrm{~kg}$, is moving in a region containing a uniform magnetic field $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{B}}=-0.4 \hat{\mathrm{k}} \mathrm{T}$. At a certain instant, velocity of the particle is $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{v}}=(8 \hat{\mathrm{i}}-6 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+4 \hat{\mathrm{k}}) \times 10^6 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$ and force acting on it has a magnitude 1.6 N . Answer the following :Question:Which of the following is correct?
options: motion of the particle is non-periodic but y and z - position co-ordinates vary in a periodic manner
motion of the particle is non-periodic but x and y - position co-ordinates vary in a periodic manner
motion of the particle is non-periodic but x and z - position co-ordinates vary in a periodic manner
motion of the particle is periodic and all the position co-ordinates vary in a periodic manner
questions.source: Most Important Selected Qs for JEE Advanced

questions.subject: Physics
questions.examName: Most Important Selected Qs for JEE Advanced
questions.q: Paragraph:Two long parallel wires carrying currents 2.5 amp and I amp in the same direction as shown in figure. An electron moving with a velocity $4 \times 10^5 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{sec}$. as shown in figure and experiences a force of magnitude $3.2 \times 10^{-20} \mathrm{~N}$ at the point R . ThenQuestion:The value of current I is
questions.solution:
$F=q v B \sin \theta=q v B\left(\theta=90^{\circ}\right)$
So, $B=\frac{F}{q v}=\frac{3.2 \times 10^{-20}}{1.6 \times 10^{-19} \times 4 \times 10^3}=5 \times 10^{-7} \mathrm{~T}$
Now, $\frac{\mu_0}{2 \pi}\left[\frac{I_p}{5}+\frac{I_q}{2}\right]=5 \times 10^{-7}$
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{I}=4 \mathrm{amp}$
If the distance of point $R$ from third current carrying current is $X$, then
$\begin{aligned} & B_R=0 \\ & \frac{\mu_0}{4 \pi} \frac{2 \times 2.5}{|\times|}+5 \times 10^{-7}=0 \\ & \text { so } x= \pm 1 \mathrm{~m}\end{aligned}$
questions.source: Most Important Selected Qs for JEE Advanced

ThenQuestion:The position between PR at which a third long parallel wire carrying current 2.5 amp may be placed such that magnetic induction at R is zero.
options: at 1 m from R and the direction of current is inward.
at 1 m from R and the direction of current is outward
at 1 m from P and the direction of current is inward.
at 1 m from P and the direction of current is outward
questions.solution:
$F=q v B \sin \theta=q v B\left(\theta=90^{\circ}\right)$
So, $B=\frac{F}{q v}=\frac{3.2 \times 10^{-20}}{1.6 \times 10^{-19} \times 4 \times 10^3}=5 \times 10^{-7} \mathrm{~T}$
Now, $\frac{\mu_0}{2 \pi}\left[\frac{I_p}{5}+\frac{I_q}{2}\right]=5 \times 10^{-7}$
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{I}=4 \mathrm{amp}$
If the distance of point $R$ from third current carrying current is $X$, then
$\begin{aligned} & B_R=0 \\ & \frac{\mu_0}{4 \pi} \frac{2 \times 2.5}{|\times|}+5 \times 10^{-7}=0 \\ & \text { so } x= \pm 1 \mathrm{~m}\end{aligned}$
